So welcome back to module eight of straight line persuasion. I want to just take a moment to congratulate you for really getting to this point to module eight. It's an accomplishment and you deserve to give yourself a pat on the back. You're almost in the home stretch here. And I think you see now that the things that you're learning are not, you know, on the drawing board is some concept. These are real world strategies that you can use with, you know, absolute, you know, deadly effectiveness. And when I say deadly, I don't mean that to make people dead, to hurt people. I'm talking about it to get people what they want and to be ethical and honest. And that's always, you know, you'll hear me repeat that time and time again. And it's because it's so crucial to take these skills to empower people. Now in module eight here, you're going to learn something called boxing someone in. That's really why I started off that way. Because boxing someone in, what it means is that you actually eliminate all their reasons not to buy and have them admit that they should be buying. And step by step, you're creating that emotional and logical box for them. You have four sides and there's really no reason they know it themselves in order to really say no now. They have to go against their own beliefs, things they know about in society, about what's right and wrong. And when you do that, unless you're actually helping someone make the decisions they should be making, it's wrong. So you're never going to box someone in unless they should be buying your product or should be buying into your idea or your concept. You'll also learn how to position the three tens in a specific order and engage in a process which is called looping. And that means if one of the tens is missing, you'll know it is by the way because they won't be buying, you're going to learn how to actually navigate your way around the straight line and what's called loops until you actually get those three tens in one moment in time and you have squashed the buying beliefs using precise language patterns which you're going to learn right now and you'll see how when you do all those together, people simply buy because there's no reason not to. Just so you know, before I come on stage and speak, I fire off what's called an anchor. This is something I was taught in the neurolinguistic program. It's very powerful. And what it is, anchoring is a process where you actually can learn to trigger a state in an instant and you change your physiology radically by going back to a moment in time when you felt a certain way and associating some gesture or either a kinesthetic squeezing your fist, a sound, a smell. Smell, I'll get to that in a second. You can recreate a physiology like Pavlov's dogs. You know Pavlov's dogs? They rang the bell every time they gave the dog food, the dog would salivate. Soon enough, they'd ring the bell, the dog would salivate anyway. It's anchored into the sounds of the bell. If right now, all of a sudden you hear a police siren and before your conscious mind even be aware, your heart will be beating faster. That's an anchor for you. A police siren is anchor. We're anchored all the time into things. You're anchor set all the time. We live our lives with anchors. Just mostly by accident. The key of advertising is to anchor you into a certain state. We've been taught since we're this big to wade it out, to negotiate, it's gotta be some time, it's gotta pass, you gotta do the work. We've been taught not to buy like that. Now I'm a pretty instant buy, I buy quickly, but I still don't buy like that. It takes a little bit of work to get me to buy. I've been conditioned that way. Okay, and this is what I've been saying all along, is embracing the power of no. This, my friends, is when the sale starts. These loops, visually, what they are, what it really means is that if you're here on the line, and they say, let me think about it, you cannot go forward. You can't say fuck that. I'm gonna try to close you anyway. Next close, you can't do that. A loop means you stop, you go backwards, and you take another shot at creating the three tens and you move back to the line and you go forward, and your loop, does it end here? No, your loop ends a little further down the line. So if your loop's a little bit, that's not a perfect visual there, but does your loop, you go back, you go online, and you end a little bit further and ask for the order again. Makes sense to everybody? Your next loop, you're gonna go here, you're not gonna go forward, you go whoop, back, and loop back around again. Notice the loop is small, and the first loop, the loop gets tighter. The metaphor is not a noose around this neck, so please don't think, that's not what I mean, okay? When I say tighter, I mean the language pattern gets more tight because A, you've already pretty much established the first one. By the way, if you can't establish that your products are 10 for this person, you get a brand new product to sell. Everyone needs to have that concept, or sell something else, or you're speaking to the wrong people, you've qualified the wrong people, okay? So the loop gets tighter because what's happening is, you're talking less and less about the product, and more and more about yourself, and the direct benefits to him. Your self and your integrity, and I say myself, I don't mean talking about me, me, me, no. Your integrity in how you handle people, and your company's integrity, and what you could do for them besides this one transaction over the long term. This is about helping people overcome obstacles to buying, to motivate them to take action when they should. Help them break through those fear barriers, the things that we generalize. Remember I told you about deletion, distortion, and generalization, that's how we make sense of the world, right, what happens is, is when we were five and our mommy said, you know, don't trust a stranger, don't take candy, all this sort of stuff, we generalized it out to everything, don't trust anybody. Now in this world we live in today, you can't live like that, you can't live in a bubble, you just can't, you're getting barrage with emails and offers and texts and this and that, you gotta exist in the world, move through it without fear. You have to make smart decisions, but you have to make decisions quickly in this world, or you get destroyed, financially, emotionally, it doesn't work. So the strategy of saying I'm not gonna trust anybody is a very disempowering strategy, we generalize that from when we were kids and also for many people they were burnt by inscrubulous salespeople, which makes your job as an honest salesperson hotter. Fact is though, because they were burnt once, does that mean that all salespeople are evil, yes or no? Makes me hungry. Breastings in sliced bread, okay? Which leads us to step two. Okay. Selling yourself. How do you sell yourself? You don't just say, I'm the best, I'm the greatest, trust me, trust me, trust me, trust me. You sound like a fool and a salesman. Many, many years ago they had this core, like Dale Carney was one of these courses, and you said someone's name five times, they trusted you, people are a bit wiser than that right now. You know the days of, few guys have been around at all, that these Ben Franklin closes, you know, Ben Franklin closes, you'd say, well, when old Benjamin Franklin couldn't make a decision, he was a very wise man, he did something very simple. He took out a big piece of paper, he drew a line right down the middle, and he put on one side of the paper, all the positives why you should buy the product, and all the negatives on the other side, why you shouldn't buy the product, and whichever side had more of the positives and negatives, he did that thing. And of course you guide them through, and you come up with 84 positives and two negatives. Well people are way too smart for Ben Franklin closes right now. However, the theory of a Ben Franklin closes is exactly what you're doing as you're moving down the straight line. You're just doing it subtly and elegantly, and it happens in a loop why you're breaking beliefs. So you have Ben Franklin closes is pure logic, pure logic, it's like, you know, energy expended, benefit created, risk reward, it's important, but you can't do a Ben Franklin close, people will laugh at you. Okay, so how do you sell yourself? Well, the best way to sell yourself is to make it into a metaphor, create an example that is so simple and undeniably logical that they have to admit that you're right. So what you do is this, depending on what industry that you're in, you're a stock broker, for instance, something I know real well, right? Say let me ask you a question, if I've been your broker for the last three or four years, making you money on a consistent basis, but you went to four or five stocks, you made money on all of them, you wouldn't be saying let me think about it right now, you'd probably be buying the stock, right? What's he gonna say that one? He could say three things, and this is the line now, watch the straight line, this three responses, now I'll switch to red. I like red. So remember that looping exercise that you just saw on video, you just watched, remember that you only loop when it's in someone's best interest and you only box someone in when they really ought to be buying your product. One of the things I want you to focus on here, remember, is tonight, go through this exercise where you identify your own physiological triggers. What actually allows you to become certain, meaning I want to get certain, I tap my hand like this, some people will point their finger like this, I don't recommend you point at certain people because they might smack you, but you're gonna find that if you want to whisper, it's very if you narrow your eyes, it becomes much easier to whisper. If you want to create this feeling of, you know, you're not looking to really, you know, invade someone's privacy, so you want to get that disarming tone, you might find by pushing your arms out, it becomes easier to develop that tone. So here would be an example. If you were gonna say this so when you know, you know, how much money do you have in the stock market? Right? You might have problems saying that in the right tone, people do. So you would say, you know, how much money do you have in the stock market? And put your arms out and you might find it helps you develop a disarming tone. So go through some of the tonal patterns and I want you to notice as you do the tonal patterns, what do you do with your body? What do you do when you sound certain? And you'll learn that you can actually trigger a certain tonality by leading with your body. The two are connected. You have the mind and the body, the tonality and the gesture. So learn that skill, it's very powerful. You'll see some more of it later on. But most importantly right now, I want you to take what you just learned to heart. I want you to practice and I want you to learn to master the art of boxing someone in with integrity. Which means only when they should be boxed in because they need to make a decision because it empowers them and helps their family.
